``FileStorage.fileinfo`` was a public API that people might have used in their code, we cannot throw it away without a `DeprecationWarning` and a deprecation phase. It should at least wrap the `FileInfo` class and provide backward compatibility with tests that ensure backward compatibility for a few releases.

Also a class that you create just to call a single method should probably just be a function :)
See https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o9pEzgHorH0 for a nice discussion around that topic.

By the way, my only request is that `FileInfo` becames an internal class (`_FileInfo`) used only by ``FileStorage.fileinfo`` and everything else keeps working around the ``FileStorage.fileinfo`` method. That will give us time to deprecate it in the future while and think a clean and long-term interface for `FileInfo` class while being able to merge the change without too much changes.

In the future I suggest you try to keep a single change in each pull request, having a single huge pull request that: adds a new feature, changes an interface and removes a public api changing 5-6 classes using it makes pretty hard to check the pull request and merge it safely. It would have been easier to manage doing one step at time in different pull requests :D

That "about 1 minute" period of time sounds like it might be related to the port being in one of the [`CLOSE_WAIT` or `TIME_WAIT` states](https://superuser.com/a/173542/48014).

To test that hypothesis, I'd start by checking whether the port shows up in `netstat -n | grep _WAIT` while the problematic behaviour is occurring.

@jd @harlowja Yes, ideally projects shouldn't need to test the retries at a level that requires a mock of time.sleep. However it is not uncommon to have complex configuration/custom functions that makes it less straightforward. In that case testing the overall behaviour is necessary if only to prove that our configuration of tenacity will do what we think it will do (e.g. we are not holding it wrong).

Mocking before the underlying code imports tenacity seems like an OK solution but its implementation certainly isn't obvious as having to do something like that is extremely rare IMO. 

Perhaps a good compromise is to add tests to the project that do the kind of mocking of time.sleep you are describing (for both traditional unitest.TestCase and async style tests) and link to it from the documentation.

First, be sure that the device is really the one you want, z.devices is a list of all devices
Asking z.devices[0] means the first device of the list
You can use z.get_device_from_addr() to get a particular device

If there no endpoints on z.devices[0].endpoints, maybe you need to refresh information
```z.devices[0].refresh_device()```
Calling ```z.devices[0].get_property('current_level')``` supposed that the value `current_level` as been already fetched by calling read_attribute_request or by device self reporting
Current_level is coming from cluster 8, attribute 0 so you have to call `z.read_attribute_request(ADDR, ENDPOINT, 8, 0)` (you have to specify the addr and the endpoint)

I think the point is that if user uses `BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy` provided by asyncio, the connection pool is still thread safe (because `BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy` actually doesn't share a event loop but create a new one in the thread) and thus `StrictRedis` is also thread safe.

At first i thought if the user customizes his own event loop policy using `AbstractEventLoop`, it may cause problem because their code may not be thread safe. So we should abandon loop argument in connection pool and use `asyncio.get_event_loop` to provide event loop for each connection separately.

But then i realized, if user customizes their event loop policy and change the `policy.get_event_loop`, even the `asyncio.get_event_loop` will not be thread safe.

I think we should not assume the point that if someone explicitly defines a loop, they are expecting everything to work in one thread. 
Insteadly, I think we should assume that the event loop users given is thread safe(since the default event loop is thread safe) and remind our users in the doc of the thread safe problem they may have if they customize the event loop themselves

I believe there may be some confusion here with which token you may be referring to. The tokens that this library is using are API tokens, which are more like personal access tokens (passwords that are not your actual password) rather than OAuth tokens.

Did you get your token from here or via some OAuth mechanism?

https://hashicorp.zendesk.com/agent/admin/api/settings

You can read about the auth options in the developer documentation here:

https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/introduction#security-and-authentication

Which shows that the token usage by this library is still correct, and the OAuth token mechanism is different. At this time OAuth is not supported, but it should probably be added at some point.

Yes, the plan is to have a consistent interface, but using makefiles instead of build scripts as that makes windows-compatibility easier.

The makefiles will have three targets
build: to build the simulation model or FPGA image depending on tool type
run: to run simulations. Not used for synthesis tools and such
run-gui: Run simulator tool in GUI mode, if there is one.

There should probably be a build-gui or similar to fire up the synthesis/P&R tool in GUI mode as well.

The makefile will also contain the default values of the runtime arguments, but these will be possible to overwrite in the FuseSoC run functions.

"a timeout value" is a timeout for `Scheduler._load_tasks`? It may not a reliable solution as it depends on the performance of database.

To build a large capacity scheduler, memory is the limitation. There are two aspect for the limitation. First, users always prefer lower resource consumption. Second, the design of scheduler, tasks of one project must be stored in memory of the same host. That's say, even if you can distribute different project to different hosts, the tasks of the same project is limited by the memory capacity of a single server (about hundred millions). Only if design a scheduler that can distribute the tasks to different instance (which is too complex for pyspider).

Another limitation is the performance of database. Reflected in the time of loading tasks into memory and the performance of query and update new requests.

Currently solution is to use `priority` and handwritten script to reduce the tasks in ACTIVE status and the number of new requests. With distributed mongodb cluster, one of the project in a pyspider instance has 300 million tasks in it (but not more than 1 hundred thousand tasks in ACTIVE).

But if you do want to have that large number of ACTIVE tasks, not loading them into memory in one-time is a reasonable solution as the tasks couldn't be fetched in one day. It's used in the spider of Baidu too. We select the tasks in order of priority with the number that can be done in 24 hours every day. You can design such mechanism too.

I believe that  this code faithfully implements the key localization specified in the  https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-reeder-snmpv3-usm-3desede-00 draft standard for 3DES, which is why it is no surprise that it works with Extreme Networks and also Cisco.    

I want to point out that all third party tools I used (SnmpGet SnmpSoft, SNMP++) worked fine with 3DES for both Cisco and Extreme networks because they implemented the reeder draft standard. It is only in AES192/AES256 encryption that problems arise because Cisco did not follow the bluementhal draft standard (instead, used the key localization from the 3DES draft standard).

If I understand you correctly, and we wrote a game of "pong" on the terminal, you print term.clear() before drawing each new frame, and this makes the screen (and probably the ball, too) appear to flicker like candles, very unattractive !

This will definitely flicker, curses doesn't flicker because it doesn't actually clear the screen, it has a "virtual screen" that it manipulates and compares to what was previously drawn -- if we moved the ball by only one column, then it would need only erase one character, and draw one character, it does not need to erase the entire screen.

You can see in example program, https://github.com/jquast/blessed/blob/master/bin/worms.py#L197 that I simply erase precisely what I expect to erase, its quite easy for a "worms" game.

For pong, you could simply draw blank over the old ball before drawing the new. You can also buffer this as a single string, and by printing it at one time as a single print() command, the terminal evaluates it so quickly that you do not notice the flicker.

I ended up creating my own parser and transformer and added it to our stack. Some points that may wanna know for applying transformation to Wavefront OBJs (if you don't know already):
- `v` lines should be transformed with the result of `tmat4x4` * `x y z [w=1]`. There might also be extra `r b g [a]` values at the end and they should stay untouched (This is already working I guess, at least partly)
- `vn` lines should be transformed with the result of `rmat3x3` * `i j k`
- `vt` lines should not be touched, just relay to export.
- `f` lines should not be touched. And for textures to work properly, they need to be in `x/x/x y/y/y z/z/z`
- There are references to material files in the source OBJ, they also need to relayed to export.

Hi,

I'm afraid the current implementation of the cpe library about WFN to URI conversion is wrong. In the second step of the section 6.1.3.1 called "Summary of algorithm", the standard specification ([NISTIR-7695-CPE-Naming](http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistir/ir7695/NISTIR-7695-CPE-Naming.pdf)) says:

> For URI components 1-5 and 7, decode the string and set the corresponding WFN attribute value.
> Decoding entails: converting sole "" to ANY, sole "-" to NA, adding quoting to embedded periods
> and hyphens, and decoding percent-encoded forms.

Therefore, **the part, vendor, product, version, update and language components must always appear
in the WFN although they are empty**. The edition component WFN value depends on if its URI value is packed or not.

For example, the correct WFN of URI "cpe:/a" is:

```
wfn:[part="a", vendor=ANY, product=ANY, version=ANY, update=ANY, edition=ANY, language=ANY]
```

not:

```
wfn:[part="a"]
```

**It's necessary to review the implementation of converter from URI to WFN with CPEs version 2.3.**

For instance, I saw that in the [line 383 of cpe2_3_uri module](https://github.com/nilp0inter/cpe/blob/develop/cpe/cpe2_3_uri.py#L383) there is a wrong continue statement (the attribute is not set) instead of setting ANY value with "v.append(CPEComponent2_3_WFN.VALUE_ANY)".

In the case of binding a WFN to a URI some components can disappear if they have empty values. For example:

```
wfn:[part="a",vendor="microsoft",product="internet_explorer",version="8\.0\.6001",update="beta",edition=ANY]
```

binds to the following URI (edition part is missing because trailing colons are removed):

```
cpe:/a:microsoft:internet_explorer:8.0.6001:beta
```

I hope my explanation helps you @TauPan. Thank you very much for your pull request and your tests.

Hey @adferrand 
I see what you're gettin at. My idea with the `--auth-*` parameters was to semi-secure credentials so that they didn't leak into log files or bash history. 

Rather than rename the `--api-protocol` flag, I think there are 2 more generic solutions:

1. Allow all flags to be passed via environmental variables.
2. Modify the certbot and dehydrated hook scripts to take in a `LEXICON_OPTIONS` environmental variable that can be used by users to pass in arbitrary custom parameters. 

eg. 
```

Thank you for your bug report. Yes, the HTTP endpoint is not used by this python module. What you see is one of the bugs in the [piligh library](https://github.com/pilight/pilight) causing SEGFAULTS. Theoretically a SEGFAULT on the server side should never be triggered by a client message.
This bug is not the only error, depending on the string you send the [pilight library](https://github.com/pilight/pilight) reproducibly crashes. Fortunately this python module allows you to send only "valid" strings, besides the new bug you have found now ;-) This cannot be fixed in this python module and is addressed in the pilight library [here](https://github.com/pilight/pilight/commit/e3d44db2da429013b2c20d2852515e0d4577926f).

The main issue is, your `pgmagick.so` isn't linked against GM C++ library, you need to add `-lGraphicsMagick++` to your LIBS in `src/Makefile`

Next difference is that on my system libgraphicsMagick.so loads multiple external codecs for various image formats - but maybe you have it statically linked (makes sense for sandboxed environment). I recommend to test reading / writing an common image format (e.g. PNG or JPG) after sucessfull importing python module to check the GM library build.

As to the "add/remove" instead of "set" API: I think I will *not* do this -- it doesn't play nicely with the way Tor works anyway. Instead, we could add some helpers in e.g. `txtorcon.attacher.*` like `PriorityAttacher` that *itself* can handle having multiple `IStreamAttachers` with associated priorities etc.

Then, you get the add/remove API by creating a `PriorityAttacher` (which would have `.add() .remove()` or similar) and you `.set_attacher()` that object into your protocol. This would all be considered "advanced usage" anyway as I *believe* the `.stream_via` and `.web_agent` calls cover most real use-cases.

Don't worry, this is how the system already functions. There's a maximum time allowed to pass between key events to consider it part of the same hotkey (controlled by the `timeout` argument, 1 second by default), and pressing any unexpected key (such as "C" being unexpected in "Ctrl, Ctrl") invalidates the state.

The change will not be counting repeated keys, but listening for key *up* events to trigger intermediate state changes (with the final event type depending on `trigger_on_release``). This will be a minor change, and mostly imperceptible to the user because intermediary state changes are not observable.

This code takes `.json` created using `labelme` as input and outputs object instance masks in binary `.png` images and saves both images and their masks in a neat folder structure. This code is adapted from `label2voc.py` by wkentaro. This code is more suitable for people using Mask-RCNN. If you have any queries ask me. 
 
```
import glob
import json
import os
import os.path as osp

import numpy as np
import PIL.Image
import cv2
import labelme

DATA_DIR = '/home/war/Downloads/initial_experiment/train'
OUT_DIR = DATA_DIR + '/data_and_masks'
class_names = []
class_name_to_id = {}
for i, line in enumerate(open(DATA_DIR + '/labels.txt').readlines()):
	class_id = i - 1  # starts with -1
	class_name = line.strip()
	class_name_to_id[class_name] = class_id
	if class_id == -1:
		assert class_name == '__ignore__'
		continue
	elif class_id == 0:
		assert class_name == '_background_'
	class_names.append(class_name)
class_names = tuple(class_names)
print('class_names:', class_names)
out_class_names_file = osp.join(DATA_DIR, 'class_names.txt')
with open(out_class_names_file, 'w') as f:
	f.writelines('\n'.join(class_names))
print('Saved class_names:', out_class_names_file)

if osp.exists(OUT_DIR):
	print('Output directory already exists:', OUT_DIR)
	quit(1)
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR)
for label_file in sorted(glob.glob(osp.join(DATA_DIR, '*.json'))):
	with open(label_file) as f:
		base = osp.splitext(osp.basename(label_file))[0]
		data = json.load(f)
		img_file = osp.join(osp.dirname(label_file), data['imagePath'])
		img = np.asarray(PIL.Image.open(img_file))
		lbl = labelme.utils.shapes_to_label(
			img_shape=img.shape,
			shapes=data['shapes'],
			label_name_to_value=class_name_to_id,
		)
		instance1 = np.copy(lbl)
		instance2 = np.copy(lbl)
		pos_1 = np.where(lbl==1)
		pos_2 = np.where(lbl==2)
		instance1[pos_2] = 0
		instance2[pos_1] = 0
		instance2 = instance2 / 2

		instance1 = instance1*255
		instance2 = instance2*255
		os.makedirs(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base))
		os.makedirs(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base,'images'))
		PIL.Image.fromarray(img).save(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base, 'images', base + '.png'))
		os.makedirs(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base, 'masks'))
		cv2.imwrite(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base, 'masks', base + '_instance1' + '.png'), instance1)
		cv2.imwrite(osp.join(OUT_DIR, base, 'masks', base + '_instance2' + '.png'), instance2)
```

> But since there might be more than one intermediates, I feel that this is best left for the management application like debops.pki to deal with, which can be programmed to recursively parse the certificates and download intermediate certificate chain as needed.

This puts a huge burden on any code that wants to interface to acme-tiny. There's no way to automatically download intermediate if you only have the leaf cert—x509 doesn't store the url to download the intermediate in the cert, just the cn. They *only* way to reliably get the intermediate is by reading the ACME `Link` headers.

For this reason, this functionality absolutely belongs in acme-tiny (and any other program that implements the ACME protocol).

Whether it should be controlled by options is a different story which I am less opinionated about (aside from there needing to be some way for acmi-tiny to just shove everything into one file). Do you have an example of an application that requires the intermediate cert to be in its own file?

So I understand the question, you want to hit the endpoint and receive back a response that looks something like this:

    {
        “access_token”: “<TOKEN>”,
        “expiration”: “<TIMESTAMP”,
    }

This is a requested feature. See #13 and #14. Unfortunately, there is not yet an implementation for this. Although, I have been working on a solution on a separate project. I will probably add this ability in the near future.

Until that point, one option would be to extract the expiration time of the access token by the client from the payload. 

Here is an example of how I did something similar in my JavaScript front end client using [momentjs](http://momentjs.com/) and [jwt-decode](http://momentjs.com/).

    import jwt_decode from ‘jwt-decode’
    import moment from ‘moment’

    const token = response.data.access_token
    const payload = jwt_decode(token)
    const expires = moment.unix(payload.exp)
    console.log('expires', expires)

I think it's going well so far! This is the syntax I'm using at least for now to define a test:
```python
@pdtFixture('deltas.yml', ['integer_values'])
def test_integer_value_deltas(cal, phrase, sourceTime, target):
    assert cal.parse(phrase, sourceTime) == (target.timetuple(), 2)
```

The `pdtFixture` decorator has a few arguments – the data file, the groups to pull from that data file, and an optional locale. If unspecified the test will be parametrized against this test data in all locales. 

In this example, the deltas tests use time offsets as targets in YAML rather than explicit dates. The `target` provided by this decorator is calculated by adjusting the `sourceTime` with `timedelta` and `Calendar.inc` for these delta times. When writing the tests in python you don't need to put as much effort into calculating or manipulating dates since it is all expressed in the YAML files.

So where do these `cal, phrase, sourceTime, target` arguments come from? `pdtFixture` inspects the decorated function to grab its argument list and prepares data based on whatever the test needs. This avoids maintaining a separate comma-separated list of parameters like for `pytest.mark.parametrize`. The order of arguments in the test function does not matter since pytest will provide them by name, and additional parameters could be provided with a separate parameterization. For example, we could parametrize prefixes and suffixes:
```python
@pdtFixture('simple.yml', ['simple_times'])
@pytest.mark.parametrize('prefix,suffix', (('"', '"'), ('(', ')'), ('[', ']')))
def test_times_with_common_prefixes_and_suffixes(cal, phrase, sourceTime, target, prefix, suffix):
    modphrase = u'%s%s%s' % (prefix, phrase, suffix)
    assert cal.parse(modphrase, sourceTime) == (target.timetuple(), 2)
```

The above example shows using the `target` parameter then formatting it into a parse response. The parametrization could also provide `targetContext` where the YAML is consulted for information about what the `pdtContext` should be or use that wildcard context if the test doesn't specify.

I originally had `parseTarget` as a parameter to provide the value of `(target.timetuple(), 2)` as an argument to the test but I think that crosses the line of too-much-magic. It's probably too abstract to just write tests like `assert cal.parse(phrase, sourceTime) == parseTarget` versus at least a bit more explicit `assert cal.parse(phrase, sourceTime) == (target.timetuple(), targetContext)`

The test failures are very clear with this approach, all the arguments are clear and the failure is shown with a diff:

```
================================================================================================ FAILURES =================================================================================================
_____________________________________________________________________ test_integer_value_deltas[cal2-2 days ago-sourceTime2-target2] ______________________________________________________________________

cal = <parsedatetime.Calendar object at 0x105f82510>, phrase = '2 days ago', sourceTime = datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 1, 0, 0), target = datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 30, 0, 0)

    @pdtFixture('deltas.yml', ['integer_values'])
    def test_integer_value_deltas(cal, phrase, sourceTime, target):
>       assert cal.parse(phrase, sourceTime) == (target.timetuple(), 2)
E       assert (time.struct_..._isdst=-1), 1) == (time.struct_t..._isdst=-1), 2)
E         At index 1 diff: 1 != 2
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_delta.py:9: AssertionError
```

If one parametrized python test function has 10 successes you'll see *1 passed* for that test, but if one python test function has 5 successes and 5 failures you'll see *5 failed*. The successful cases for a function with failures are not displayed at least by default.

One minor issue of that snippet is it will always run whenever I enter the subfolders, which will unnecessarily deactivate and reactivate the env. 
My two cents on the improvement:

```
recent_env=$(pwd)
while [ "${recent_env}" != "$HOME" ];do
    if [ -f ${recent_env}/.env ];then
        return
    fi
    recent_env=$(dirname "${recent_env}")
done
source deactivate
``` 
This will make sure the `source deactivate` runs only when there are no other .env files along the path except the one on the $HOME.

Hi Rowan!

Thanks for the pull request. The thing is, those URLs are (as muffl0n said) protocol-relative links. More infromation about them is here: https://blog.httpwatch.com/2010/02/10/using-protocol-relative-urls-to-switch-between-http-and-https/

The idea is that they work whether you serve the generated page over both HTTP and HTTPS. The downside is that they don't work when you access the page via `file:///` links. We could do what your PR does and hardcode in either http or https, but that'll stop the resources from working on certain browsers (chrome) if the page is served over the other protocol. 

There are two solutions to this problem:

You can serve the page over a HTTP server. Python has a built-in one that'll do just that:

    $ ansible-cmdb out/ > overview.html
    $ python -m SimpleHTTPServer
    Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8000 ...

You can now access the file with the http://127.0.0.1:8000/overview.html URL. The other option (which isn't included in packages releases yet!) is to supply the `-p local_js=1` commandline parameter to make ansible-cmdb generate file:/// links in the output page. This'll require the javascript files to be present in `/usr/lib/ansible-cmdb/static/js/`. These files will be automatically installed from version 1.4 of ansible-cmdb.

Hope that helps!

Regards,

Ferry

I use this library to mantain my dotenv files across a number of projects, but I have a problem with Docker and it's that for Docker an envfile gets parsed as if you split the lines by the equal sign, everything before the equal is the variable name, the rest is the value, the problem is that you end up with quotes in your values which breaks some applications.

I guess changing the behavior by default could be a breaking change, maybe a good compromise is to support a flag to set the behavior:

~~~
 --quote=all (current behavior)
 --quote=off (never add quotes)
 --quote="auto" (only add quotes for values containing spaces)
~~~

I would add the "auto" behavior to support sourcing dotenv files from bash, i use this a lot while debugging, the problem with unquoted strings containing whitespace is that bash thinks you are trying to run a command.

Maybe my tips would be usefull for someone.

Requirements: visual studio, cmake

1. download nanomsg c sources.
2. build nanomsg c sources (as described on nanomsg website (http://nanomsg.org/development.html).
i.e.:
  make build directory in nanomsg sources dir
  run cmake .. from build dir
  build nanomsg sources 
    in my case I'v made it from command line:
    First I had to run vcvarsall.bat (which resides somewhere in visual studio directory)
    and I build library by runing: msbuild nanomsg.sln /t:Build /p:Configuration=Release

after this step you should find nanomsg.lib and nanomsg.dll in Release (or Debug) directory.

3. copy nanomsg.lib to your <python>\libs directory
after this step simillar patch should exists in your system: c:\python27\libs\nanomsg.lib

4. make nanomsg directory in your <python>\include directory.
5. copy nanomsg c sources to your <python>\include\nanomsg
after this step simillar patch should exists in your system: c:\python27\include\nanomsg\nn.h

6. finally try to install python nanomsg: python setup.py install

now you can start to use nanomsg in python - BUT YOU NEED: nanomsg.dll in directory from which you run script (nanomsg.dll was created in 2.).
If there is no nanomsg.dll you will get exception after import nanomsg (WindowsError: [Error 126] ....).

If it's working you can delete <python>\libs\nanomsg.lib file and <python>\include\nanomsg\ directory

It would be nice to make it easier - maybe someone knows easy way to make binary package installer with nanomsg.dll included.
If you like to I can send you binary installer of python module "nanomsg-1.0.win32-py2.7.exe" (made with python setup.py bdist --format=wininst) and a nanomsg.dll.

Ok it's now much better.
I'v made windows installer with dll included as @shluvme suggested:
setup.py:
```python
data_files=[('lib/site-packages',["../nanomsg/nanomsg.dll",])]

setup(
    name='nanomsg',
    version=__version__,
    packages=[str('nanomsg'), str('_nanomsg_ctypes'), str('nanomsg_wrappers')],
    data_files = data_files,
```

dll would be copied to python site-pakcages.
To make sure that it's directory would be included in system PATH I'v added following code to nanomsg/\__init__.py:

```python
from __future__ import division, absolute_import, print_function, unicode_literals

from .version import __version__
from struct import Struct as _Struct
import warnings

#-----HERE:-------
import distutils.sysconfig 
import os 
os.environ['PATH'] += ';'+distutils.sysconfig.get_python_lib() 
#-----------------

from . import wrapper
```

This way everytime nanomsg is imported the site-packages are added to system path and nanomsg.dll can be found by wrapper.
Installer is made with `python setup.py bdist_wininst`.

Well, the `NoTranslationError` is a subtype of `AttributeError`. That means the following will work:
```python
    # Simply using getattr with a default
    self.__original_address = getattr(self, 'address', None)

    # Using hasattr
    if hasattr(self, 'address'):
        # do something here

    # Catching the exception itself
    try:
        self.__original_address = self.address
    except AttributeError:
        # handle error here, or just pass to ignore
```
You might also want to try out this one:
```python
    self.__original_address = self.safe_translation_getter('address', None)
```
This one is guaranteed to never perform a query to the database, simply using currently loaded translation, if any, but never attempting to check the database. If you're simply caching stuff, it might be a better option than `getattr`.

By the way, completely unrelated to hvad and your question, I think there are some gotchas to be aware of when caching attributes the way you are doing it. Namely, if you run a multi-process or multi-thread server, your cache might be out of sync at the time you actually save your instance.
Just a thing to be aware of :)

There is no support for deleting a current, active blackout period using the `alerta` command-line tool (you can "purge" old ones though) however you could delete an active one by using the API directly like this ...

```
$ curl -X DELETE http://alerta-api.herokuapp.com/blackout/5f61efd0-171b-4483-be30-8161ac716735 -H 'Authorization: Key 74tqZG8Zkfo29_k5Q7YOsXtvl7PVzTSPF-Sp7ibX'
```
You need a read-write API key and need to know the blackout period id which you can get from when the blackout period was created or by listing all the existing blackout periods.

See http://docs.alerta.io/en/latest/api/reference.html#blackout-periods for more info

class NDArrayInitializer(HasTraits):

	a = NDArray()

		self.shape = kwargs.get('shape', None)

```
transfer.upload_file(file, bucket.name, key)
```

Can we please have the ability to add headers to pre-signed urls in boto3 just as is demonstrated above in the old boto methods? 

I have a situation where I'm using a request to API gateway to run a Lambda function which generates a data file. This file is then uploaded to s3 since it is too large to return directly via the gateway. I generate a signed url and return a 302 redirect with this url as the location. 

It works fine in boto3 as long as the customer doesn't call the api endpoint with a Content-Type header but if they do that header causes the presigned url to be invalid as it is passed along with the redirect. This is very much a real world situation where a Content-Type header would be passed to a pre-signed s3 'get' request url (even though it isn't needed it is there from the original API request).

So then the question becomes - why with backports.unittest_mock 1.2 does the issue not occur? With 1.2, the `backports` module doesn't appear in the `AssertionRewritingHook._must_rewrite`, probably because `backports` was declared as a `namespace_package` in setup.py, but [with 1.3 it is not](https://github.com/jaraco/backports.unittest_mock/commit/c84284022a198d560e685c5a687458a5be4c5fe6).

This leads me to believe that any package that installs backports.unittest_mock and imports any backports module in its tests or under its tests will fail with this same error. Normally I'm a fan of small, incremental improvements.

Unfortunately, in this case, I think that we need a comprehensive re-design to make user education tractable.  If we fix a sprawling core interface like `IRequest` in little dribbles like this, I fear that we'll just end up with 19 different ways to get `.args`, each one behaving slightly differently (although slightly better than the last!), which is actually a *worse* situation than one bad interface where everyone knows the pitfalls to work around.

So, given @wsanchez's work on #181, which I think has a better hope of becoming

# “The New Request Interface For Twisted”

I think we would, sadly, be better off abandoning this work.

Nevertheless, thank you, @notoriousno for submitting it.  This is definitely a problem area and one that it's tough to work on.

There are two ways.

- You can [explicitly specify that these are plain strings at source level](http://nodeca.github.io/js-yaml/#yaml=LSAhIDIwMDEtMTItMTVUMDI6NTk6NDMuMVoKLSAnMjAwMS0xMi0xNVQwMjo1OTo0My4xWicKLSAiMjAwMS0xMi0xNVQwMjo1OTo0My4xWiI=).

Thanks @mirca - three quick comments below. 

* `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` needs to have `psfmodel` and `fitter` keywords, right?  If we're going to strictly use the "daophot algorithm", actually we should be using a custom fitter that does the linearization trick that make DAOPHOT fast, but that's pretty tied to a specific PSF model, so I think that's not critical - that is a reasonable thing to leave up to the user.  But right now I think it will just fail without a model, right?
* Shouldn't `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` be a subclass of `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry`?  That way users understand that they *can* mess around with it if they really want to, and any methods we might add to `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` down the road (e.g. better treatment of uncertainties or intermediate values) will automatically appear in `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry`.  I think the only thing you'd need to change here is `self._phot_obj` -> `self`, and the last line in `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry.__init__` needs to change to ``super(DAOPhotPSFPhotometry, self).__init__(...)``
* I realize now I wasn't too clear about the point of this... but our thinking was that `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` should *never* allow `finder` to be `None`.  That's very much an intended restriction, because it helps users understand the difference between `BasicPSFPhotometry` and `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` - the former is for "single loops" and the latter is meant to be used iteratively.  It looks like your current implementation allows `finder` to be `None` if `niters=1`, which is precisely the situation we wanted to avoid.
* This isn't absolutely necessary, but the reasoning behind having `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` use `BasicPSFPhotometry` instead of the other way around is that `BasicPSFPhotometry` is supposed to be a straightforward thing that a novice-to-intermediate user can look at and understand.  In this PR, that's a bit harder because the user has to basically understand `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` to understand `BasicPSFPhotometry`.

Hi @ErenPhayte !
Franky speaking, I'm sure what `all the documents` means, but there is a massive amount of papers and books on these matters in the web.
What you are describing in your message are two quite different classes of computer vision problems: object detection, and object segmentation. For the latter we have plenty of different methods already implemented (see http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/api/skimage.segmentation.html), although the detection is typically a more complex task and, at the moment, is presented only by http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/segmentation/plot_label.html and https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/1570.
If you introduce additional constraints to you image acquisition system, you could potentially solve the described tasks using simpler multi-stage pipeline.

Don't hesitate to look through our comprehensive gallery of examples: http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/ . Probably you could find the solution to your problem there.

You can also search the web using these keywords, investigate OpenCV algorithms, ITK, ImageJ, etc.

P.S. GitHub is not the best place to post the questions, we use it mostly as a bug-tracker and for other matters of development. For general questions, please, consider using our mailing list (https://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/scikit-image) and/or StackOverflow (most of us monitor it for questions tagged with `scikit-image`).

Should they be generated by the server at runtime?

It is supported, by adding a `data` parameter for the function call, for example:
`
client = Mollie.API.Client()
client.payments.refund(payment, {"amount": 10})
`

This might be kind of confusing, given that for other methods, the behavior is like this:
`
client.payments.all(count=20)
`

The key here is that POST requests use the first syntax, GET requests the second. This will be corrected in the next release, such that all calls can make use of keyword arguments.

Someone else can verify this work, but I believe this to be true.

In the worst case, when the data is highly uncompressible, the overhead comes from meta data to describe raw blocks. We compute the overhead by the followinG:

The stream header has:
```
7b  WINDOW_SIZE (best case is 1b, worst case is 7b. This setting is independent of input size)
```

The overhead to encode each raw block is:
```
1b           ISLAST
2b           MNIBBLES (4..6)
4b*MNIBBLES  MLEN (1..16777216)
1b           ISUNCOMPRESSED
7b           PADDING (may be less depending on bit alignment)
8b*MLEN      DATA
```

The last block has:
```
1b  ISLAST
1b  ISLASTEMPTY
```

Thus, we produce the following table:

Max input size (<=) | Max compressed extra bytes
------------ | -------------
1048576 | 5
16777216 | 6
SIZE | 1 + 5*⎡SIZE/16777216⎤ + 1

These numbers, of course, assume that the encoder falls back to using raw encoding when it detects that its compressed output is worse than just encoding it raw. I cannot confirm that the C encoder does this.

The confusion here is the two arguments `replace` and `override` 

Say they release FOAF 2.0 (Now based on google+? :) ) and you want to change `foaf` to point to `http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/`. Up to now you had `foaf=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1` and `foaf2=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0`

Now you want to "upgrade" to FOAF 2.0 :

`g.namespace_manager.bind('foaf', Namespace('http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0'), .... )`

Adding:
* `override` will do what you want, and replace the current binding for `foaf` to be the new URI. 
* `replace` will replace *any other bindings pointing to the same namespace*, so the old `foaf2` binding will be removed. 

In the FOAF2 example, you probably want both.

>  In a real scenario, profiling shows that drawImage dominates over any calculations in RandomWalk

Maybe with complicated PSFs where the PSF is dominating the time for `drawImage`, but for just the RandomWalk part of the calculation, the construction time is more than the drawing time, even for the default `nstep=40`.  

Here is a quick script the draws 100 x 100 galaxies consisting of a RandomWalk with `npoints=100` convolved with a simple Gaussian:
```
import galsim
import time

nx = 100
ny = 100

rng = galsim.UniformDeviate(1234)
psf = galsim.Gaussian(sigma=0.5)

stamp_size = 64
image = galsim.Image(100*stamp_size, 100*stamp_size)

t1 = time.time()
rw_list = [ galsim.RandomWalk(100, 2.5, flux=23, rng=rng) for k in range(nx*ny) ]
t2 = time.time()

gal_list = [ galsim.Convolve(rw, psf) for rw in rw_list ]
t3 = time.time()

k = 0
for i in range(nx):
    for j in range(ny):
        b = galsim.BoundsI(i*stamp_size+1, (i+1)*stamp_size, j*stamp_size+1, (j+1)*stamp_size)
        gal_list[k].drawImage(image[b])
        k = k+1
t4 = time.time()

image.write('rw.fits')

print 'Time for making 10,000 RandomWalk objects: ',t2-t1
print 'Time for convolving 10,000 RandomWalk objects: ',t3-t2
print 'Time for drawing 10,000 RandomWalk objects: ',t4-t3
```
The output on my laptop is:
```
Time for making 10,000 RandomWalk objects:  134.190493107
Time for convolving 10,000 RandomWalk objects:  0.139138936996
Time for drawing 10,000 RandomWalk objects:  87.2488839626
```
So I think it's worth making at least some simple optimizations to avoid the python-layer overhead that is causing most of the slow down.

> I'm not up on python3. What's the issue with StringIO?

It's only that with Python 3 `str` and `StringIO` use Unicode encoded strings, i.e., the Python 2 `unicode` type. So with Python 3 you have to distinguish whether you want to operate on text strings (Python 3 `str` type which is Python 2's `unicode`) or binary strings (Python 3 `bytes` type which is Python 2's `str`). The legacy code might use `StringIO` in binary string contexts where you'd have to change that to `BytesIO` for Python 3.
The gist is that to port Python 2 code to Python 3 you have to closely look at the intended uses (text vs. binary) of string types (`str`/`""`/`''`/`StringIO`).

As for `lav` and `bx-python` in general: TBH, I don't really know much about the project and was only carried away when trying out some Cython-related things 😉. I was made aware of the missing Python 3 support by https://github.com/bioconda/bioconda-recipes/issues/2288 and https://github.com/bioconda/bioconda-recipes/pull/6619.

Looks like this is a profiling error, not an allocation pattern. Here's a test that measures the process size after instantiating a large quantity of objects:

```python
import sys
from pympler import asizeof
from pympler import process
from pympler.process import ProcessMemoryInfo

class Empty(object):
    pass

print("Test sizing objects without attributes...")
z = Empty()
a = Empty()
print("Empty dict size:          ID={} {}".format(id({}), sys.getsizeof({})))
print("Dict size before setattr: ID={} {}".format(id(a.__dict__), sys.getsizeof(a.__dict__)))
for i in range(10000):
    setattr(a, "c"+str(i), i)

pmi = ProcessMemoryInfo()
before = pmi.vsz

b = Empty()
c = Empty()
print("Dict size after setattr:  ID={} {}".format(id(a.__dict__), sys.getsizeof(a.__dict__)))
print("Dict size other instance: ID={} {}".format(id(b.__dict__), sys.getsizeof(b.__dict__)))
print("Dict size third instance: ID={} {}".format(id(c.__dict__), sys.getsizeof(c.__dict__)))
print("Dict size predecessor:    ID={} {}".format(id(z.__dict__), sys.getsizeof(z.__dict__)))
print("\nInstantiating 100000 instances ...")
many = [Empty() for _ in range(100000)]

pmi.update()
print("VSZ delta:                {} KB".format((pmi.vsz - before) // 1000))
print("Recursive size:           {} KB".format(asizeof.asizeof(many) // 1000))
print("VSZ per instance:         {} B".format((pmi.vsz - before) // 100000))
```

And the output on my box:

```
Test sizing objects without attributes...
Empty dict size:          ID=140057526516616 240
Dict size before setattr: ID=140057526516616 112
Dict size after setattr:  ID=140057526516616 87448
Dict size other instance: ID=140057495989272 87448
Dict size third instance: ID=140057495988984 87448
Dict size predecessor:    ID=140057495988768 87448

Instantiating 100000 instances ...
VSZ delta:                6844 KB
Recursive size:           8751224 KB
VSZ per instance:         68 B
```

So roughly 68 bytes were allocated for each additional instance. If the flatsize of 87448 bytes was correct, this number should be much bigger.

This only happens when calling `setattr` on objects without attributes. If you set an attribute on any instance before or after calling setattr, the sizes appear to be correct. It looks like Python chooses a more compact format for attribute storage if there are no attributes set on instances of a class. 

I suspect a copy-on-write optimization which doesn't work correctly (for `sys.getsizeof`) when using `setattr`.

If I remember correctly, the reason we added this `_shape_fix` is that without it, MXNet cannot infer the shape of the h2h normalization (since the initial state of the LSTM is created somehow with delayed shape information: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/rnn/rnn_cell.py#L433)
So the hack we did was to use `zeros_like` to get the shape of the input (`i2h`) at runtime. For efficiency we wanted to do this only for the first call to the cell when being unrolled, but we never checked whether this actually gives any gains.

Maybe the right way would be to always add this shape_fix.

Hello Evelio,

Huston, we have a problem. If you look at the following command:

```
route 198.51.100.101/32 next-hop 198.51.100.2 label [ 800002 ] bgp-prefix-sid [ 300, [ ( 800000,4096 ), ( 1000000,5000 ) ] ];
```

It is decoded as:
```
route 198.51.100.101/32 next-hop 198.51.100.2 label [ 800002 ] bgp-prefix-sid sr-label-index 300, sr-srgbs [(1000000, 5000), (800000, 4096)]
```
which in turn can not be re-parsed by ExaBGP. The format must be bi-directional. Could you please change this ?

Also the JSON:
```
{ "exabgp": "4.0.0", "time": 1495359723.9699042, "host" : "ptr-41.212.219.82.rev.exa.net.uk", "pid" : 19802, "ppid" : 13720, "counter": 1, "type": "update", "neighbor": { "address": { "local": "127.0.0.1", "peer": "127.0.0.1" }, "asn": { "local": "65000", "peer": "65000" }, "direction": "in", "message": { "update": { "attribute": { "origin": "igp", "local-preference": 100, "bgp-prefix-sid": { "sr-label-index": "300", "sr-srgbs": "[(1000000, 5000), (800000, 4096)]" } }, "announce": { "ipv4 nlri-mpls": { "198.51.100.2": [ { "nlri": "198.51.100.101/32", "label": [ 800002 ] } ] } } } } } }
```
The prefix part:
```
"bgp-prefix-sid": { 
   "sr-label-index": "300", 
   "sr-srgbs": "[(1000000, 5000), (800000, 4096)]"
}
```
 sr-label-index is a number, not a string and sr-srgbs is an opaque string. The JSON should be fully parsed to not required any hard work from clients.

I would really appreciated if you could update the patch to reflect the change outline here please.

Thank you very much for your work and help.

@marekjez86 You should set the `styleMapFamilyName` custom parameter on the instances that you wish to define a custom nameID=1 or family name (it will be used on legacy apps that only support the four regular-bold-italic-bolditalic style names).

Basically, the lint tool is checking that the family name (nameID=1) does not exceed 31 characters length for all the fonts in the family.

For the phase 3 Noto fonts, the data file `nototools/data/family_name_info_p3.xml` contains entries for each group of fonts, including Tamil, e.g.

```xml
  <info family="sans-taml" family_name_style="short" use_preferred="t" />
  <info family="sans-taml-ui" family_name_style="very short" use_preferred="t" />
```

The `noto_names` script has a dump command that you can feed a font or a directory of fonts and will write to stdout the expected name table data. For example:

```bash
$ python nototools/noto_names.py dump -p 3 -f ../noto-source/instance_ttf/NotoSansTamil-SemiCondensedThin.ttf

using name info file: "[tools]/nototools/data/family_name_info_p3.xml"

Personally, I like separating tests from "real code". Some people prefer the opposite.

But it might make the whole asyncio vs twisted vs generic test-organization a lot nicer to do something like:

* autobahn/* (existing code, as now)
  * twisted/*.py
  * asyncio/*.py
* tests/* (move test-code to here)
  * /generic/test_*.py
  * /asyncio/test_*.py
  * /twisted/test_*.py

I'm not sure if this would remove *all* the hack-ish things to get around test-collection etc. That is, trial would still have to be "tricked" into not importing ``tests/asyncio/*`` unless we make ``tests/*`` "not a module" (i.e. no ``__init__``). Then we could be explicit when running things and eliminate the environment-variables (i.e. run like ``trial tests/generic tests/twisted`` or ``py.test tests/generic/ tests/asyncio``).

There's also the "distribute the test-code with your module or not" question (e.g. that's one of the reasons people prefer tests "in" with the code, so they always come along). Since Autobahn has "small" systems as a target audience this might be a good reason to *not* include that code at all (i.e. you only get the ``tests/*`` code if you do a Git checkout; a ``pip install`` wouldn't include them).

Maybe for this PR @izderadicka's original solution is really the best? :/

...and we could consider a wholesale move of the tests to their own tree as a separate ticket?

We had somehow similar issues with requests, keep-alive and broken connections. We had http client, REST server and IPVS balancer in between:
```
client <-> balancer <-> server
```
After connection being quiet for a while balancer simply forgets about established TCP connection and subsequent request after a long pause results in RST being received on client side. It is the same as in-flight-FIN race being discussed here.
`requests` does it's best to check if TCP connection is still alive - it calls `poll()` with 0 timeout to check connection status upon taking connection from pool, but if client hasn't yet received FIN/RST from server it rightfully assumes that connection can be used and issues HTTP request.

This problem could be worked around by enabling `SO_KEEPALIVE` on socket, but:
  * Race condition would still be possible
  * There's no interface to set in `requests`

Our solution was to setup `HTTPAdapter` in requests so that `connection pool size` is less than `retries count`. This works like this, suppose we have connection pool size = 2 and retries count = 3. We take first connection from pool and request fails with socket error, we take second connection from pool and it fails too, so we create new connection and it works (or doesn't, but that's another case).

I took a look at your implementation and as you don't use retries, so these errors are expected and should be handled explicitly.

Hi there! From the code snippet you provided, I am afraid that I am not even sure if you are using turbodbc ;-). I have no idea what the `exasol` instance is supposed to be, and neither `do_INSERT` nor `commit_QUERY` are methods provided by turbodbc.

Assuming above snippet uses turbodbc one way or the other, you are essentially creating a new statement for each line you insert. This is not very efficient and might lead to the error message you experience, though I assume that this is a limitation of the Exasol ODBC driver.

With plain turbodbc, you could probably avoid the error message and be more efficient with this snippet:

```
query = "INSERT INTO {1}.{2} ({3}) VALUES ({4})".format(project.staging_table_name,
                                                        target_table,
                                                        ','.join(header),
                                                        ','.join(['?' for _ in header]))

connection = turbodbc.connect(dsn="My Exasol connection")
cursor = connection.cursor()
cursor.executemany(query, reader)
connection.commit()
```

This builds the query _once_, and transfers parameters in bulk via `executemany` in a single statement. Since `reader` is an iterable over parameter sets, it is directly usable in `executemany`.

`__init__.py` is the common entry point for the "generated" entry points that go in `bin`/`Scripts` as well as the `python -m conda` entry point provided by `__main__.py`.

The location of the change in this PR is as far up in the call stack as possible without creating duplicate code or forcing us to write custom entry points (which I also don't want do).  The structure of entry points have a huge WTF history with stupid stuff setuptools has done in the past, and I'd rather keep the WTFs to a minimum, even if justified.

Sorry I'm late to respond here.  And I can't believe I was able to get this so wrong in so many places.  `CONDA_VERIFY_SSL` is *wrong*.  It must be `CONDA_SSL_VERIFY`.

    CONDA_SSL_VERIFY=false conda update pyopenssl

Also, you must be currently upgrading from conda 4.2+ for this to work.

If you have an older version of conda, use the following

    conda config --set ssl_verify false
    conda update pyopenssl
    conda config --remove ssl_verify

I know why---it's because the `defaults` version of `traitsui` would force an upgrade to `pyface 4.6.0` or later, while the `conda-forge` version does not.

So by reaching down to `conda-forge`, it has reduced the _number_ of packages that it has to update by one. That is a higher priority objective, so that's the choice it makes.

I'm afraid this is just a corner case that we're not going to be able to fix. My bigger concern, in fact, is that conda-forge's recipe for `traitsui` seems to have no version constraints at all on its packages; and that our `traitsui` package doesn't even _have_ a `traits` dependency, which it would seem it obviously ought to.

@ellisonbg see #657

When I run python, the python executable in serverA:/pathA/condaA/bin loads into the memory on serverB and runs there. If I type "which python" on serverB, it shows me ServerA:/pathA/condaA/bin/python. If I run python on serverB and look at top, I see the process running on serverB. However, the binary executable is on serverA.

For stability, we need to run jupyter from serverB:/pathB/condaB/bin. However, if I open a notebook and type "!which python", I need to see "/pathA/condaA/bin/python", not "/pathB/condaB/bin/python."

The reason for this is that serverA:/pathA/condaA/ is on the drive attached to our cluster. This is where we keep all of our software packages. serverB is a machine for development and interactive processing for jobs that may later be run on the cluster. So, they need to be developed with the exact same binaries as we would run on the cluster.

We could have a process to regularly match up condaB with condaA. But it would be much simpler to have jupyter simply call the binary in /pathA/condaA/.

I only speak from a "customer's" perspective now: I am using schematics to validate incoming request data in a Flask application. I want to be strict with data - a string needs to be a string, an int needs to be an int, and so on. This is both to avoid accidentally introducing potential security problems and to keep the API as straightforward and simple as possible. I don't want to run in those scenarios were client accidentally sends us a number, and then we process that as a string, resulting in something else than what client expected.

I do see it now that I should have read the project description better - maybe schematics just isn't intended for this use? Anyhow, I feel the documentation is a bit lacking in this sense - the docs do hint about type coercion, but an example or two (or the table from #364) would do better to enlighten reader's awareness of the data casting.

> schematics is about importing data from possibly untrusted sources and making it fit into a schema, by doing the necessary lossless conversions when possible.

You put it nicely there, @lkraider. I would love to see this under the project description.

> StringType().to_native(True) returns the string "True", which I think is just too surprising

I would say this is correct, if the input was `"True"` and not `True`. The problem (for me) is that `"True"` is accepted in the first place.

> One thing I'd love to hear people's thoughts on is the relative importance of controlling type strictness at data import time as opposed to field definition time. Should we have both? If yes, how should the settings interact?

I just changed the code a little bit to get color for the methods:
```
from androguard.misc import AnalyzeAPK
from androguard.core.analysis.analysis import ExternalMethod
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import networkx as nx

a, d, dx = AnalyzeAPK("4e2201cde26141715255d2421f0bcfb1")

CFG = nx.DiGraph()

# Note: If you create the CFG from many classes at the same time, the drawing
# will be a total mess...
for m in dx.find_methods(classname="Lcom/elite/MainActivity;"):
    orig_method = m.get_method()
    print("Found Method --> {}".format(orig_method))
    # orig_method might be a ExternalMethod too...
    # so you can check it here also:
    if isinstance(orig_method, ExternalMethod):
        is_this_external = True
        # If this class is external, there will be very likely
        # no xref_to stored! If there is, it is probably a bug in androguard...
    else:
        is_this_external = False

    CFG.add_node(orig_method, external=is_this_external)

    for other_class, callee, offset in m.get_xref_to():
        if isinstance(callee, ExternalMethod):
            is_external = True
        else:
            is_external = False

        if callee not in CFG.node:
            CFG.add_node(callee, external=is_external)

        # As this is a DiGraph and we are not interested in duplicate edges,
        # check if the edge is already in the edge set.
        # If you need all calls, you probably want to check out MultiDiGraph
        if not CFG.has_edge(orig_method, callee):
            CFG.add_edge(orig_method, callee)

pos = nx.spring_layout(CFG)

internal = []
external = []

for n in CFG.node:
    if isinstance(n, ExternalMethod):
        external.append(n)
    else:
        internal.append(n)


nx.draw_networkx_nodes(CFG, pos=pos, node_color='r', nodelist=internal)
nx.draw_networkx_nodes(CFG, pos=pos, node_color='b', nodelist=external)
nx.draw_networkx_edges(CFG, pos, arrow=True)
nx.draw_networkx_labels(CFG, pos=pos, labels={x: "{} {}".format(x.get_class_name(), x.get_name()) for x in CFG.edge})
plt.draw()
plt.show()
```

That was an interesting tests, that come with a few of caveats: I had to modify the b2 startup script, I had to reinstall b2 and the problem is still there using python3.

To get python3 on my machine I decided to use pyenv, which keeps track of which version of Python you want to run based on a file it places in the local directory.  If there is no file, you run the 'global' version of Python (2.7.11 in my case).

    brew install pyenv
    pyenv install 3.5.1
    cd <directory where I run b2 from to do a sync>
    pyenv local 3.5.1

I added the required `$(pyenv init -)` to my .bash_profile, opened a new bash session and went to the correct directory.  When I started a sync, `ps -ef` showed me that the 2.7 version of Python was still being used.  The problem was the b2 startup script.

The /usr/local/bin/b2 script on my machine started with the line:

    # !/usr/local/opt/python/bin/python2.7

I changed it to this get it to work with the "current" version of Python:

    #!/usr/bin/env python

The first time I got b2 to run with python3, it complained about not having the site-packages in the 3.5.1 directory.  So I reinstalled b2 from the same directory marked for use with python3:

    pip install b2

This time b2 started fine with python3, as shown by `ps -ef`:

    501 47816 47730   0 11:25AM ttys003    1:10.27 /Users/dwisehart/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/bin/python3.5 /Users/dwisehart/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/bin/b2 sync /Volumes/BackupOld/ b2://DW-Backup/Vol-BackupOld/

You can see from that it is running python3.5 but you can also see that it is running a new b2 startup script that, sure enough, has the version hard-coded in the script again:

    #!/Users/dwisehart/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/bin/python3.5

My suggestion is that you switch to using `#!/usr/bin/env python` on the systems that support that type of startup.

Unfortunately, after trying this test, `b2 sync` is stuck in exactly the same place it was before

     compare: 257287/257485 files   updated: 0 files   0 B   0 B/s

`ps -ef` verifies that python3.5 is being used and the runtime is not growing in this stuck state.

These use cases are helpful and cover a pretty broad range. I think that properly used IANA timezones (the pytz/pandas convention) can cover all except for solar time. For example, here in the US we have ``US/Mountain`` (DST aware) and ``MST`` (no-DST):

```python
pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T00']).tz_localize('US/Mountain')

<class 'pandas.tseries.index.DatetimeIndex'>
[2015-04-01 00:00:00-06:00]
Length: 1, Freq: None, Timezone: US/Mountain


pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T00']).tz_localize('MST')

<class 'pandas.tseries.index.DatetimeIndex'>
[2015-04-01 00:00:00-07:00]
Length: 1, Freq: None, Timezone: MST
```

Note the difference in the UTC offset between the two.

So long as I specify times consistent with the timezone, pandas will use the same integer to represent them:
```python
# US/Mountain
pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T01']).tz_localize('US/Mountain').astype(int)

array([1427871600000000000])

# MST
pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T00']).tz_localize('MST').astype(int)

array([1427871600000000000])

# UTC
pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T07']).tz_localize('UTC').astype(int)

array([1427871600000000000])

# No specification
pd.DatetimeIndex(['2015-4-1T07']).astype(int)

array([1427871600000000000])
```

So I think we already have all of the tools we need, and the ``Location`` objects and the ``solarposition`` module already make use of these tools.

On solar time... maybe I see part of the remaining problem now. We've been talking about a way to go from a UTC-based timestamp to solar time (e.g. ``solarposition.ephemeris``), but you want to be able to do the reverse. That seems harder. Could be doable with interpolation. Maybe @alorenzo175 knows how to do it exactly.

(no newlines) → inline:

(no first newline, yes last newline) →  undefined:

One more nitpick:

```
if hasattr(reloc.owner_obj, "guess_simprocs"): 
```

I'd rather see `guess_simprocs` exists as a property for the `Backend` class in CLE, so we don't have to test the existence of this property (which is not a programming style that we follow).

Personally, I prefer splitting it into two properties: `guess_simprocs` specifying if we want to enable SimProcedure guessing for this backend, and `guess_simprocs_hint` (or `guess_simprocs_hints`) provides a hint (or some hints) for the guessing procedure. Alternatively, you may disable the SimProcedure guessing logic when `guess_simprocs` is `None`. Either way, we should give it a clear definition of what the desired behavior is, without testing the existence of this property.

Your TEST3 is the correct output. There are a couple things going on here!

- It's so long and complicated because of how every single thumb instruction can be conditional.
- It's also very long because you're using vex without optimization enabled. `pyvex.set_iropt_level(1)` will simplify things drastically.
- It's only lifting a single instruction because vex only works on the level of basic blocks. That means that whenever it encounters an instruction that branches or jumps or calls or returns somewhere, it'll end the block after that instruction.

MPRIS integration is badly broken with this patch (but you probably already know). What is very problematic is that we use mpv's JSON IPC to get real playback status from the player. This is the only way how to get true player status since user can control the player via keys (or even maybe via JSON IPC API). This can't be done in current approach.

I suggest to not touch MPRIS part (at least for now). It runs in separate process and connects to player independently. Just tell it if it should expect mpv or mplayer.

btw I think it might be reasonable to just support mpv. It is clearly better for us than mplayer (JSON IPC API). It should be supported everywhere mplayer runs. Since mps-youtube now only works reasonably with mpv or mplayer I think it might not be a great loss, but that of course needs some discussion. Users can use NOTIFIER to run custom scripts.

Hi @zsert3 
`points` frame behaves like the depth frame it was created from: It contains the array of stacked rows. 
`++` will bring you to the next point to the right, unless you reach of of row, then you will jump to start of next row. Some points are going to be `(0,0,0)`. We keep those to allow easy navigation within the point-cloud grid (you can access point corresponding to pixel (i, j) using `v[j * width() +i]`)

Vectorized environment is a wrapper for multiple environments running in parallel (so that an algorithm can get updates from multiple trajectories at once - this gives larger update batches and reduces the variance of the updates). SubprocVecEnv uses environments running is separate subprocesses that communicate with head process via pipes. ShmemVecEnv is largely a duplicate of SubprocVecEnv, we'll be cleaning that up at some point.

Agreed, there is no issue with pybind11 here.

The main issue is that any time you go from c++ code back to Python (whether explicitly calling a Python function, or implicit operations such as creating new Python variables), you have to take out a gil lock.  So as long as each wrapper function that the external library calls does this (by keeping a `gil_scoped_acquire` instance alive for the duration of the Python interaction), everything should be fine.

That's a good idea, but:

* the various scriptworkers have to verify other scriptworker instance types. for instance, a balrog task needs to verify upstream, which includes beetmover and signing.  beetmover needs to verify signing. we can require that each scriptworker instance type installs all the upstream scriptworker script modules, but that gets a little unwieldy.  plus, there has to be logic to figure out which implementation to use to verify each instance type.
* similarly, `verify_cot` runs locally against any task in the graph, so you would have to install every scriptworker module locally to be able to run it and verify all upstream tasks of any arbitrary scriptworker task.
* i think it's good to have all the cot verification in one place, where we can inspect it and audit it and spin it out into its own module.

If we're able to make all the scriptworker task definitions to look the same (we might already be there once they're all ported to use scriptworker 1.0.0), maybe we only have to verify it's a scriptworker task, and we don't care what type during cot verification.  that would simplify things.

Hi!

I've taken a look at this, and it echoes an ongoing discussion on the django-developers mailing list: should we provide for a ``get_first_or_create``, ``get_last_or_create``, or some other logic?

The best option I can think of right now would be to add a hook where the user could add some custom code to choose which duplicate to use:

```python

class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = models.User
        django_get_or_create = ['firstname', 'lastname']
        django_queryset_chooser = lambda qs: qs.latest('pk')  # Default would be lambda qs: qs.get()
```

Side note: if you wish to use a custom DjangoOptions in your project while this API is designed, you could use the following code (not part of the officially supported API though):

```python

import factory.django

class MyDjangoOptions(factory.django.DjangoOptions):
    # Your overrides here

class MyDjangoModelFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    # Use our custom "options" class instead of the default.
    _options_class = MyDjangoOptions


class UserFactory(MyDjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = models.User
        # This is now supported!
        django_get_first_or_create = ('firstname', 'lastname')

```

@guoyuhong thanks for raising these issues. These are great points to discuss.

First, regarding a job API. What are the requirements? Something like Spark submit (https://jaceklaskowski.gitbooks.io/mastering-apache-spark/spark-submit.html)? What else?

Second, regarding object eviction from the object store, it would be great to understand when LRU is not good enough. What workload (it looks to me that for the graph workload LRU should be fine, but maybe I'm missing something) ? 

Of course, by LRU I mean that the objects will be evicted according with the times since they've been last accessed (red/write). And if we have workloads for which LRU is bad, one question here is whether configuring the eviction policy per driver/job would be good enough.

Also, related to the above we need to add the ability to spill over the objects to a persistent store. If you guys have some thoughts here please let us know. Also, this would be a great task to collaborate or maybe for you to lead.

Added more tests. I'm sure we could add even more, but the tests do push at some of the nastier edge cases now (including background tasks crashing at different moments), and the coverage report is solid.

Also, I added some code to use friendly names for all the fixture tasks, which could hopefully help with this issue (https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/47#discussion_r204700827):
> The great thing I find about this is you don't end up with a root nursery containing all the coroutines which make make it much harder to tell which one is responsible when your test hangs with a deadlock.

In the course of this (and also while thinking about https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/607), I have started to think that maybe the details of our new cancellation rules aren't *quite* right: I'm leaning towards saying that when a fixture crashes, we should cancel all "downstream" fixtures, but not do anything at all to independent fixtures. Right now, we only cancel the test, not other downstream fixtures... except that we also cancel all fixtures that haven't started yet, including independent ones. This means that if you have two independent fixtures that both crash during setup, you'll actually get different exceptions on different runs, randomly, depending on which one happens to run first.

But these are some pretty obscure details that aren't going to really matter to 99.9% of users, and this PR is already out of control, so I'd rather merge this as is and then open an issue to keep track of that as a possible followup change.

The bug will indeed be the dropping of the dependency in #155 - without that backport specified, you have to have something else somewhere in your dependency tree specifying the inclusion of futures or it won't work on 2.7.

tornado.concurrent doesn't patch sys.modules as far as I can (briefly) tell.

So - it looks to me like tests are passing because of this ```
Collecting futures (from tchannel>=0.27->jaeger-client==3.10.1.dev0)
  Using cached https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/2d/99/b2c4e9d5a30f6471e410a146232b4118e697fa3ffc06d6a65efde84debd0/futures-3.2.0-py2-none-any.whl
```

that is the jaeger-client[tests] depends on tchannel depends on futures.

Any non-test install will be failing at the moment.

I suggest reinstating the futures dependency :).

I think you removed that because of compat concerns with setuptools? Best way to deal with that IMO is to use a pyproject.toml with the setuptools version you need.

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/#specification

```[build-system]
# Minimum requirements for the build system to execute.
requires = ["setuptools>42", "wheel"]  # PEP 508 specifications.
```

But if you can't do that for some reason, we can use the pre-PEP-508 hacky approach that setuptools has supported for many many years of an extra called "" with conditions attached to it. its been a while since I had to fiddle with that (doing PEP-508 was my effort to get *away* from this mess), but its something like:

```
 extras_require={
        'test': [....],
        ':python_version<"3"': [
            'futures'
        ]
    }
```

which while ugly will work back through to insanely old setuptools.
